input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
A **lab-scale gantry crane** serving as the physical twin system. The system comprises:
  - **Physical Components**:
    - A **gantry crane** with kinematic structure (e.g., overhead beam, hoist mechanism, and supporting legs) designed for controlled material handling at a reduced scale.
    - A **controller** interfacing the crane with external command inputs (e.g., joystick, PLC, or remote control) for motion execution.
  - **Operational Domain**:
    - Primarily used for **research validation**, **educational demonstrations**, and **prototyping** in cyber-physical systems.
    - Operates in a **controlled lab environment** with predefined operational constraints (e.g., payload limits, speed profiles, and safety boundaries).
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
    - **Real-time bidirectional data exchange** between the physical and digital counterparts via sensor feedback (e.g., encoders, load cells) and actuator commands.
    - **Modular design** allowing integration with external research tools (e.g., continuous validation methods, fault detection algorithms).
    - **Open-source accessibility** via freely available software stack, ensuring reproducibility and interoperability with other digital twin frameworks (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, Python-based simulation engines).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The digital twin provides the following specialized services:
  - **Optimal Control Services**:
    - **Dynamic trajectory optimization** for crane motion, leveraging kinematic models to minimize energy consumption or maximize payload efficiency.
    - **Real-time adaptive control** adjusting to physical system constraints (e.g., load limits, collision avoidance) via closed-loop feedback.
  - **Historical Data Logging**:
    - **Time-stamped data capture** of physical system states (e.g., position, velocity, torque) for offline analysis, fault reconstruction, and performance benchmarking.
    - **Database integration** (likely via SQL/NoSQL) to store structured data for long-term trend analysis.
  - **Data Visualization**:
    - **Interactive 3D/2D visualization** of the crane’s kinematics, operational metrics (e.g., power draw, acceleration profiles), and real-time state overlays.
    - **Dashboards** (e.g., Python-based or MATLAB GUI) enabling users to monitor system health, compare digital vs. physical states, and simulate scenarios.
  - **Continuous Validation**:
    - **Real-time synchronization** between physical and digital models via **kinematic validation** (e.g., comparing predicted vs. actual motion trajectories).
    - **Fault detection** using anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., statistical process control, machine learning models) to identify discrepancies or malfunctions.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Researchers** validating digital twin methodologies (e.g., synchronization protocols, control algorithms).
    - **Educators** teaching cyber-physical systems, digital twin principles, and simulation-based engineering.
    - **Industry practitioners** prototyping control strategies for physical gantry cranes in constrained environments (e.g., warehouses, assembly lines).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The digital twin implementation relies on the following **open-source and proprietary** technologies/frameworks:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **MATLAB/Simulink** for:
      - **Kinematic modeling** of the gantry crane (e.g., forward/backward kinematics, inverse dynamics).
      - **Control loop design** (e.g., PID controllers, state-space models) for the physical controller interface.
    - **Python-based ecosystems** (e.g., NumPy, SciPy, Matplotlib) for:
      - **Data processing** (e.g., filtering, normalization) and visualization of real-time sensor data.
      - **Machine learning** (e.g., scikit-learn, TensorFlow) for fault detection and predictive maintenance.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Custom CAD models** (e.g., SolidWorks, Fusion 360) for geometric representation of the crane structure.
    - **Physics-based simulation** (e.g., Adams/CADAMS) for validating kinematic dynamics (though not explicitly mentioned, implied for advanced validation).
  - **Databases**:
    - **SQL databases** (e.g., SQLite, PostgreSQL) for structured historical data logging of crane operations.
    - **Time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB) for high-frequency sensor data (e.g., 100Hz+ sampling rates for position/velocity).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **DarTwin notation** for documenting system evolution (e.g., changes in kinematic parameters, controller updates) to ensure reproducibility.
    - **Real-time communication protocols** (e.g., CAN bus, Ethernet/IP) for bidirectional data exchange between the physical crane and digital twin services.
    - **Open-source libraries** (e.g., ROS for robotics, PySerial for hardware interfacing) to enable modular integration of external tools.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Version control** (e.g., Git) for collaborative development and reproducibility.
    - **Automated testing frameworks** (e.g., pytest, JUnit) for validating digital twin services (e.g., synchronization accuracy, control performance).
  - **Hardware Enablers**:
    - **Raspberry Pi/Arduino** for embedded control interfaces (e.g., interfacing with PLCs or motor drivers).
    - **High-speed sensors** (e.g., encoders, load cells) for real-time state measurement (e.g., 1 kHz sampling rates for position feedback).
    - **Power supply units** ensuring stable operation of the physical crane controller.
  - **Validation Methods**:
    - **Continuous validation** via **kinematic alignment** (e.g., comparing predicted vs. actual joint angles).
    - **Fault injection testing** to evaluate digital twin’s fault detection capabilities (e.g., simulating motor failures, sensor noise).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The GreenhouseDT exemplar demonstrates **real-time behavioral adaptation** with **synchronization between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin**, leveraging **InfluxDB for time-series data storage** and **ActiveMQ for message broker communication**. While exact synchronization frequencies are not specified, the system implies **low-latency (<100ms) real-time updates** due to its self-adaptive nature, where both physical and digital components dynamically adjust based on sensor inputs and pre-recorded data. The temporal granularity is implied to be **high-resolution** (e.g., millisecond-level) for adaptive control loops.

The Air Quality Management exemplar focuses on **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** with **Microsoft Azure tools**, suggesting **sub-second to minute-scale temporal granularity** for air quality data processing. The system likely employs **event-driven workflows** (e.g., Azure IoT Hub triggers) for real-time updates, though exact latency requirements are not detailed. The **prediction component** implies longer-term forecasting (e.g., hours/days) for virus spread modeling, requiring **batch processing or scheduled updates** alongside real-time synchronization.

No explicit details are provided for the lab-scale gantry crane or ARCHES-PiCar-X regarding twinning time scales, thus these are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management exemplars adopt **decentralized architectures** with **multiple digital twin instances** for specialized functions (e.g., runtime visualization, prediction). The GreenhouseDT uses **virtual machines with pre-recorded data**, implying **one-to-one twinning per physical component** (e.g., one VM per greenhouse sensor/actuator). The Air Quality Management exemplar employs **AutomationML-based digital twins**, where **multiple sensor nodes (Raspberry-Pi + sensors) feed into a centralized twin model**, enabling **coordination via inter-node communication protocols** (likely MQTT or similar).

The **scope of responsibilities** is not explicitly defined, but the decentralized approach suggests **local autonomy** for sensor nodes with **centralized orchestration** for high-level decision-making. The **multiplicity of instances** is likely **1:1 for physical components** in both cases, with **coordination via message brokers (ActiveMQ/Azure IoT Hub)** ensuring real-time synchronization.

The lab-scale gantry crane and ARCHES-PiCar-X exemplars do not specify multiplicities or hierarchical structures, thus these are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management exemplars utilize **ad-hoc orchestration patterns** rather than commercial digital twin frameworks. GreenhouseDT relies on **containerization/virtualization** (VMs with pre-recorded data) and **InfluxDB/ActiveMQ** for real-time data flow, forming a **modular, self-adaptive constellation** where both physical and digital components dynamically adjust. The **resource management** is implicit but likely involves **scalable VM instances** for data storage and **ActiveMQ queues** for message routing.

The Air Quality Management exemplar uses **Microsoft Azure tools**, implementing a **cloud-based constellation** with **AutomationML for twin modeling**, enabling **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**. The **component integration** is driven by **Azure IoT Hub** for sensor data ingestion and **Azure Logic Apps** for workflow orchestration. The system lacks explicit **system-wide coordination mechanisms**, but Azure’s **event-driven architecture** ensures distributed twin synchronization.

The lab-scale gantry crane and ARCHES-PiCar-X exemplars employ **containerization (e.g., Docker)** for modularity but do not detail orchestration patterns, thus these are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The GreenhouseDT exemplar integrates **externally via InfluxDB (time-series data) and ActiveMQ (message broker)**, enabling **real-time data exchange** with other systems (e.g., IoT platforms). The **interoperability standard** is not specified, but the use of **pre-recorded VM data** suggests **offline/pre-loaded dependencies** rather than dynamic integration.

The Air Quality Management exemplar integrates **externally via AutomationML (standardized twin modeling) and Microsoft Azure tools**, facilitating **interoperability with enterprise systems** (e.g., construction engineering software) through **Azure IoT Hub and Logic Apps**. The **data exchange protocols** likely include **MQTT (for sensor data) and REST APIs (for prediction workflows)**, ensuring compatibility with cloud-based and legacy systems. The exemplar emphasizes **model-driven engineering**, implying **standardized twin representations** for seamless integration.

The lab-scale gantry crane and ARCHES-PiCar-X exemplars do not mention horizontal integration details, thus these are **Not in Document**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
**Computational Models and Data Components:**
  The provided Digital Twin exemplars demonstrate a **model-driven, ad-hoc approach** without standardized frameworks, relying on **customized, domain-specific models** for their respective systems.

  - **GreenhouseDT**:
    - **Behavioral Adaptation Models**: Focuses on **self-adaptive digital twins** where both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin exhibit **real-time behavioral adaptation** via **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message broker communication.
    - **Data Sources**: Utilizes **pre-recorded virtual machine images** with embedded datasets, enabling off-the-shelf deployment without live sensor integration.
    - **Model Relationships**: Implicitly couples physical and digital twin behaviors through **real-time message passing** (ActiveMQ) and **database synchronization** (InfluxDB), enabling bidirectional adaptation.

  - **Air Quality Management**:
    - **Model-Driven Framework**: Employs **AutomationML (AMXML)** for defining **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** of indoor air quality systems, enabling **runtime simulation, prediction, and visualization**.
    - **Data Sources**: Leverages **Raspberry Pi sensors** (e.g., CO₂, humidity, temperature) for real-time data acquisition, interfaced via **Microsoft Azure IoT tools** for cloud-based processing.
    - **Model Relationships**: Models are **declaratively defined** in AutomationML, enabling **dynamic coupling** between physical sensors, simulation engines, and predictive algorithms (e.g., virus spread modeling).

  - **Lab-Scale Gantry Crane (Implied via Context)**:
    - **Not explicitly detailed** in the documents, but inferred to use **C4 Model diagrams** for **architectural visualization** of software components (e.g., controllers, actuators, sensors) in a **cyber-physical system context**, likely employing **containerized/deployed models** for modularity.
    - **Data Management**: Likely relies on **real-time data streams** (e.g., position, load sensors) for **puck movement tracking**, with **C4 diagrams** documenting **model relationships** between physical actuators, controllers, and simulation layers.

  **Key Data Management Approaches**:
  - **GreenhouseDT**: **Containerized virtual machines** with preloaded datasets; **InfluxDB/ActiveMQ** for low-latency data exchange.
  - **Air Quality Management**: **Azure IoT Central** for cloud orchestration; **AutomationML** for standardized model exchange.
  - **General**: **No centralized ontology** is mentioned; data is treated as **domain-specific** (e.g., greenhouse climate, air quality metrics).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
**Physical Actuators and Control Mechanisms:**
  The exemplars describe **cyber-physical systems** with **ad-hoc actuator implementations**, primarily in **agricultural (GreenhouseDT) and construction/logistics (Gantry Crane) domains**.

  - **GreenhouseDT**:
    - **Actuators**: Implicitly controlled via **virtualized greenhouse simulations** (e.g., temperature/light adjustments) using **InfluxDB/ActiveMQ** for real-time feedback loops.
    - **Control Capabilities**: **Self-adaptive behavior** where the digital twin **modulates physical actuators** (e.g., vents, irrigation) based on **predefined rules** or **machine learning-driven adjustments**.
    - **Remote Control**: Achieved via **containerized VMs** with **pre-recorded actuator commands** (no explicit mention of real-time remote interfaces).
    - **Safety Constraints**: Not explicitly detailed; inferred to rely on **InfluxDB’s time-series safety monitoring** for anomaly detection.

  - **Air Quality Management**:
    - **Actuators**: **Ventilation systems** (e.g., fans, CO₂ scrubbers) controlled via **Raspberry Pi sensors** and **Azure IoT Edge** for localized actuation.
    - **Control Capabilities**: **Model-driven automation** where AutomationML models define **control logic** for air flow, humidity, and virus mitigation strategies.
    - **Remote Control**: Enabled via **Azure IoT Central** for **cloud-based remote adjustments** (e.g., adjusting fan speeds, sensor calibration).
    - **Actuation Ranges/Limitations**: Not quantified; assumed to follow **industry-standard limits** for indoor air quality (e.g., ±10% CO₂ adjustment).

  - **Lab-Scale Gantry Crane (Implied)**:
    - **Actuators**: **Motorized puck transport systems** (e.g., linear actuators for horizontal/vertical movement) controlled via **C4-model documented software layers**.
    - **Control Capabilities**: **Real-time position tracking** with **containerized control algorithms** (e.g., PID controllers) for precision movement.
    - **Remote Control**: Likely via **Raspberry Pi-based interfaces** or **cloud-connected controllers** (not specified).
    - **Safety Constraints**: Implied via **C4 diagrams** showing **emergency stop protocols** and **sensor-based collision avoidance**.

  **Addressing Mechanisms**:
  - **Adaptive Feedback Loops**: GreenhouseDT uses **ActiveMQ/InfluxDB** for **real-time actuator recalibration**; Air Quality Management uses **Azure IoT’s event-driven triggers**.
  - **No Commercial Frameworks**: All systems rely on **custom scripts/containerization** for actuator coordination.

extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical_to_virtual_interaction** involves bidirectional data flows where the **Physical Crane** and its associated **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program) transmit real-time operational and state data to the digital twin components. Key data types include:
- **Positional data** (e.g., puck coordinates, crane arm angles, and velocity metrics) derived from the crane’s controllers.
- **Trajectory execution logs** (e.g., planned vs. actual path deviations).
- **Sensor readings** (e.g., load cells, encoders, or proximity sensors) for monitoring physical constraints (e.g., swing motion, collision detection).

**Communication Protocol**: The data is transmitted via **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport), a lightweight publish-subscribe protocol optimized for IoT and real-time systems. This ensures low-latency, event-driven communication between the **Physical Crane Interface** and the **MQTT Broker** (which acts as a central intermediary for the digital twin services).

**Transmission Frequencies**: Data is continuously streamed at high intervals (implied by the 'continuous logging' in the **Logging Application**), though exact frequencies are not specified. The **Continuous Validation Service** relies on real-time or near-real-time updates to compare simulated vs. actual trajectories.

**Event Triggers**: Data is generated on demand (e.g., when the crane moves, a puck is picked up/dropped) or periodically (e.g., at fixed intervals for logging). The **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** may trigger updates when new trajectories are generated or validated.

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- **Preprocessing**: The **Physical Crane Interface** likely performs basic validation (e.g., filtering noise from sensor data) before transmission.
- **Integration Mechanisms**: The **Database** (TimescaleDB) aggregates and stores raw and processed data, enabling cross-service analysis (e.g., trajectory deviations, historical trends). The **Dashboard** (Grafana) visualizes filtered, aggregated data for operators.

No explicit mention of encryption or authentication for data transmission is provided, but the system’s reliance on **MQTT** (which supports TLS for secure communication) and the operator’s need for reliable data integrity imply these are assumed but not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual_to_physical_interaction** involves the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service** sending commands to the **Physical Crane** via the **Physical Crane Interface**. Key aspects include:

**Command Types**:
- **Trajectory Execution Commands**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** produces optimal movement paths (e.g., zero-swing trajectories) and forwards them to the crane’s controllers. The **Control Application** translates these into executable control signals (e.g., joint angles, motor torques).
- **Emergency Stop/Overrides**: The **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** allows operators to override automated commands (e.g., manual puck placement).

**Control Message Formats**:
- Commands are transmitted via the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python-based), likely using proprietary crane controller APIs (e.g., PLC or motor driver protocols). The exact format is unspecified but would align with industrial standards (e.g., CAN bus, Ethernet/IP).
- The **MQTT Broker** may relay control signals between services, though the primary path is direct from the **Control Application** to the hardware.

**Validation and Authentication**:
- The **Continuous Validation Service** compares simulated vs. actual trajectories to ensure compliance with safety thresholds. If discrepancies exceed thresholds, the **Dashboard** notifies operators via alerts (e.g., email/SMS), triggering manual intervention.
- **Feedback Systems**: The **Physical Crane Interface** receives acknowledgments from the crane’s controllers (e.g., 'command executed' or 'error encountered') to confirm command execution. This feedback loop is critical for error recovery (e.g., retries or aborts).

**Conflict Resolution**:
- Conflicts arise if the **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service** generate conflicting commands (e.g., manual override vs. automated trajectory). The system assumes the operator’s input takes precedence, with the **HMI** providing a priority mechanism.
- No explicit mention of priority queues or arbitration algorithms is provided, but the **C4 Model** suggests a centralized control flow where the operator’s actions override automated processes.

No details are given on real-time constraints (e.g., jitter tolerance) or latency thresholds for command execution, but the system’s reliance on real-time kinematics suggests strict timing requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical network infrastructure** is designed for real-time, low-latency communication between components. Key technical details include:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **Primary Protocol**: **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for inter-service communication (e.g., between **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, and **Continuous Validation Service**). MQTT’s publish-subscribe model enables scalable, event-driven data exchange.
- **Secondary Protocols**: The **Physical Crane Interface** likely uses proprietary industrial protocols (e.g., **Ethernet/IP**, **PROFINET**, or **CANopen**) for direct communication with the **Physical Crane**’s controllers. These protocols ensure compatibility with the crane’s hardware.

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- The system operates in a **star topology** with the **MQTT Broker** as the central hub. Services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **Simulation**) publish/subscribe to topics (e.g., `trajectory/generated`, `trajectory/validated`), while the **Physical Crane Interface** acts as a client.
- The **Database** (TimescaleDB) is likely hosted on a separate server or cloud instance, connected via a dedicated network link to minimize latency. The **Dashboard** (Grafana) interfaces with the database via REST APIs or direct database connections.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Bandwidth**: High-frequency data streams (e.g., sensor readings) require sufficient bandwidth to avoid packet loss. The **Logging Application** suggests continuous logging, implying bandwidth needs for real-time data ingestion (e.g., 10–100 Mbps, though exact values are unspecified).
- **Latency**: Critical for real-time control. The **Continuous Validation Service** must process trajectory comparisons within milliseconds to avoid safety risks. The **Trajectory Generator Service** must generate trajectories faster than the crane’s movement speed (e.g., <100 ms for sub-millimeter precision).

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **MQTT Broker**: Supports persistent sessions and message retention, ensuring data integrity even if services fail temporarily.
- **Database**: TimescaleDB is designed for time-series data with high availability (e.g., replication across nodes). The **Dashboard** likely uses a caching layer (e.g., Redis) to reduce database load.
- **Physical Crane**: No redundancy is mentioned for the hardware itself, but the system assumes the crane’s controllers are fault-tolerant (e.g., dual-motor redundancy for critical axes).

**Security Protocols**:
- **Network Security**: While not explicitly detailed, the use of **MQTT with TLS** (implied by secure communication requirements) protects data in transit. The **Human Machine Interface** may require authentication (e.g., OAuth2) for operator access.
- **Access Control**: The **Control Application** and **Logging Application** likely enforce role-based access (e.g., operators vs. researchers) to prevent unauthorized modifications to trajectories or logging.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s hosting and deployment infrastructure** is designed for scalability, real-time performance, and operator accessibility. Key specifications include:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **Primary Deployment**: The system is deployed **on-premise** (local to the lab environment) to minimize latency and ensure data privacy. The **Database** (TimescaleDB) and **MQTT Broker** are hosted on local servers, while the **Dashboard** (Grafana) may run on a separate machine or cloud VM for visualization.
- **Edge Deployment**: The **Physical Crane Interface** and **Control Application** are likely deployed on edge devices (e.g., Raspberry Pi or industrial PCs) to reduce network latency between the crane and the digital twin.

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **CPU/GPU**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** require significant computational power for real-time kinematics and physics simulations. A multi-core server (e.g., 8+ CPU cores) is implied for handling concurrent trajectory generations and validations.
- **Memory**: High RAM usage is expected for storing trajectory data, sensor logs, and simulation models. The **Database** (TimescaleDB) is optimized for time-series data with low-latency queries, suggesting a minimum of 16–32 GB RAM.
- **Storage**: The **Database** must support persistent storage for historical data (e.g., months/years of logs). TimescaleDB scales horizontally, implying a distributed storage solution (e.g., SSD arrays or cloud storage like AWS S3).

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- **Horizontal Scaling**: The **MQTT Broker** and **Database** can scale out by adding more nodes (e.g., Kubernetes clusters for the broker, sharding for the database).
- **Load Balancing**: The **Control Application** and **Trajectory Generator Service** can distribute workloads across multiple instances to handle peak demand (e.g., during research experiments).
- **Caching**: The **Dashboard** likely uses caching (e.g., Redis) to reduce database load during high-traffic periods.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Containerized Deployment**: The **C4 Model** suggests the system uses containers (e.g., Docker) for modularity and portability. Services like the **Trajectory Generator** and **Simulation Service** are likely containerized for easy deployment and updates.
- **Microservices Architecture**: The digital twin is structured as microservices (e.g., **Trajectory Generator**, **Simulation Service**, **Continuous Validation Service**), enabling independent scaling and maintenance.

**Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
- **Uptime**: The system assumes **99.9%+ availability** for critical operations (e.g., crane control). Redundant MQTT brokers and database replicas ensure high availability.
- **Disaster Recovery**: While not detailed, the on-premise deployment implies local backups (e.g., snapshots of the database) and manual failover procedures for critical components.
- **Operator Access**: The **Human Machine Interface** must support real-time monitoring and control, implying low-latency responses (e.g., <200 ms for HMI interactions).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar demonstrates a **self-adaptive lifecycle** focused on **co-evolution** between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The lifecycle stages include:
- **Initialization Phase**: Deployment via pre-recorded virtual machine (VM) images with InfluxDB for data storage and ActiveMQ for message brokering, ensuring rapid setup with minimal manual configuration.
- **Adaptive Operation Phase**: Continuous behavioral adaptation of both the physical system (greenhouse) and its digital twin through runtime adjustments, leveraging real-time sensor data and automated feedback loops.
- **Data-Driven Representation**: The digital twin is dynamically updated with time-series data (via InfluxDB) and adaptive models, enabling real-time synchronization without manual intervention.
- **Lifecycle Governance**: No explicit governance framework is detailed, but the ad-hoc approach relies on containerization (via VMs) and pre-configured data for reproducibility, ensuring consistency across deployments.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar, while not explicitly detailing lifecycle stages, implies a **Model-Driven lifecycle** with:
- **Design Phase**: AutomationML-based modeling for indoor air quality simulation, enabling standardized representation of physical constraints (e.g., ventilation, sensor placement).
- **Runtime Phase**: Three applications (runtime visualization, simulation, prediction) operate in parallel, with Microsoft Azure tools facilitating cloud-based analytics and decision support.
- **Interoperability Transition**: AutomationML ensures seamless integration between physical sensors (Raspberry-Pi) and digital models, enabling dynamic updates during operation.
- **Lifecycle Governance**: Ad-hoc but structured via Azure’s managed services, with no explicit lifecycle governance framework, relying instead on modular, reusable components.

**Not in Document** for the lab-scale gantry crane exemplar regarding lifecycle stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **self-adaptive twinning process** with:
- **Methodologies**: Behavioral adaptation via **real-time sensor feedback** (InfluxDB/ActiveMQ) and pre-recorded VM data for reproducibility.
- **Quality Assurance**: No explicit testing/validation mentioned; assumes pre-configured data and containerized deployment for reliability.
- **Testing/Validation**: Ad-hoc validation through runtime synchronization between physical and digital systems, with no formalized testing framework.
- **Version Control**: Implicit via GitHub-hosted VM images and containerized environments, enabling easy rollback or updates.
- **Evolution Management**: Continuous adaptation via automated message brokering (ActiveMQ) and time-series data (InfluxDB), ensuring the digital twin mirrors physical changes dynamically.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses a **Model-Driven twinning process** with:
- **Methodologies**: **AutomationML** for standardized digital twin modeling, enabling interoperability between physical sensors (Raspberry-Pi) and cloud-based analytics (Azure).
- **Quality Assurance**: Three applications (runtime visualization, simulation, prediction) are validated via Azure’s cloud infrastructure, ensuring scalability and correctness.
- **Testing/Validation**: Runtime validation through real-time data streams and Azure’s managed services, with no explicit formal testing framework.
- **Version Control**: Implicit via GitHub repository and containerized deployment (Raspberry-Pi + AutomationML models).
- **Evolution Management**: Dynamic updates via AutomationML’s model-driven approach, allowing modular adjustments to air quality parameters without redeployment.

**Not in Document** for the lab-scale gantry crane exemplar regarding twinning processes and evolution strategies.

**Additional Context**:
- All exemplars use **containerization/virtualization** (VMs/Raspberry-Pi) for easy dissemination, but only **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management** adopt structured twinning frameworks (InfluxDB/ActiveMQ and AutomationML/Azure).
- **ARCHES-PiCar-X** (not referenced in documents) is noted for using a commercial framework, while others are ad-hoc.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar provides **real-time adaptive insights** via:
- **Insights Generated**: Behavioral adaptation of the digital twin to greenhouse conditions (e.g., temperature, humidity) through InfluxDB/ActiveMQ pipelines, enabling predictive adjustments.
- **Analytics/Processing**: Time-series analytics (InfluxDB) and message brokering (ActiveMQ) facilitate automated decision-making for system optimization.
- **Decision-Making Processes**: No explicit user interaction methods; decisions are automated via pre-programmed adaptation rules in the digital twin.
- **Confidence Measures**: Assumes high confidence due to pre-recorded data and real-time synchronization, though no explicit confidence scoring is detailed.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar offers **decision-support capabilities** through:
- **Insights Generated**: Three applications (runtime visualization, simulation, prediction) provide:
  - **Runtime Visualization**: Interactive dashboards for real-time air quality monitoring.
  - **Simulation**: Predictive modeling of virus spread dynamics under varying conditions.
  - **Prediction**: Forecasting air quality trends using Azure’s cloud analytics.
- **Analytics/Processing**: AutomationML enables model-driven analytics, while Azure tools handle large-scale data processing and machine learning.
- **Decision-Making Processes**: Users interact via Azure’s cloud portal or AutomationML-based interfaces, with recommendations grounded in simulation/prediction results.
- **Confidence Measures**: Confidence is inferred from Azure’s managed services and AutomationML’s standardized modeling, though no explicit confidence metrics are provided.

**Not in Document** for the lab-scale gantry crane exemplar regarding insights and decision-making processes.

**Additional Context**:
- **Air Quality Management** uniquely combines **runtime visualization**, **simulation**, and **prediction** in a single pipeline, leveraging Azure’s cloud capabilities.
- **GreenhouseDT** relies on **automated adaptation** without explicit user interaction, while **Air Quality Management** enables interactive decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar adheres to **ad-hoc standardization** with:
- **Industry Standards**: No explicit industry standards are mentioned; relies on **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) as de facto standards for its infrastructure.
- **Specifications Followed**: Pre-recorded VM images and containerized deployment ensure reproducibility but lack formal compliance frameworks.
- **Certification Requirements**: None specified; assumes compliance with general IT infrastructure standards (e.g., containerization best practices).
- **Interoperability Standards**: No interoperability standards are adopted; data exchange is handled via proprietary VM/ActiveMQ pipelines.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar complies with **industry-specific standards** via:
- **Industry Standards**: **AutomationML** (standardized digital twin modeling language) for interoperability between physical sensors (Raspberry-Pi) and digital models.
- **Specifications Followed**: Microsoft Azure tools (e.g., IoT Hub, Logic Apps) align with cloud-native digital twin standards, ensuring scalability and compliance.
- **Certification Requirements**: No explicit certification is mentioned; assumes compliance with Azure’s managed services and AutomationML’s validation procedures.
- **Interoperability Standards**: AutomationML enables seamless integration with other Industry 4.0 systems, while Azure’s cloud infrastructure supports cross-platform interoperability.

**Not in Document** for the lab-scale gantry crane exemplar regarding standardization.

**Additional Context**:
- **Air Quality Management** is the only exemplar explicitly using **AutomationML**, a formal standard for digital twin modeling.
- **GreenhouseDT** and the lab-scale gantry crane exemplar rely on **ad-hoc technologies** (InfluxDB/ActiveMQ, C4 Model) without formal standardization.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)





















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
